skill.name: brenda-database
skill.description: Query the BRENDA enzyme database through its SOAP API, parse enzyme records, compare kinetic parameters, and generate analysis outputs for biochemical research.
skill.summary: Query BRENDA enzyme kinetics, reactions, organisms, and pathway data.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review did not confirm malicious intent or prompt injection in the reviewed files. Most static findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, EC numbers, chemical formulas, and SHA-256 documentation. The skill still has medium risk because it uses authenticated external BRENDA access, reads credentials from environment-style configuration, and writes user-selected export files.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External API Access
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to contact the official BRENDA SOAP API and requires account credentials for requests. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it creates external network and credential handling risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly identifies a BRENDA WSDL endpoint and authenticated email/password API calls. The context is legitimate enzyme database access, not covert exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Credential Loading From Environment Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to place BRENDA_EMAIL and BRENDA_PASSWORD in a .env file or environment variables. The API reference also documents a helper that loads .env data into os.environ.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential names and .env loading behavior are explicit. No evidence found that credentials are sent anywhere except the documented BRENDA authentication flow.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected Local File Writes
medium_findings.description: Helper functions export query results and pathway reports to caller-provided filenames. This is useful behavior, but it can overwrite local files if untrusted paths are passed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and use caller-controlled filename values. The surrounding code writes analysis exports and reports, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command detections point to Markdown code fences or inline code examples. No Ruby execution or shell backtick execution is present at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation syntax and install guidance. It is not executable Ruby or shell code inside the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Biochemical Values Misclassified as IP Addresses or C2 Keywords
low_findings.description: Several hardcoded IP and C2 detections are EC numbers, molecular formulas, molecular weights, cofactors, or ordinary sorting parameters. These are domain data for enzyme pathway analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged strings appear inside metabolite and cofactor dictionaries or examples using EC number notation. No command-and-control behavior is evident from these locations.

low_findings.title: SHA-256 Documentation Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography finding points to BRENDA password hashing documentation using SHA-256. This appears to describe the API authentication requirement rather than a custom weak cryptographic design.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation names SHA-256, not MD5 or SHA-1. The reviewed files do not show a vulnerable password storage implementation.

low_findings.title: Ordinary Import Blocks Misclassified as Dynamic Imports
low_findings.description: The dynamic import findings point to standard Python import statements inside try/except dependency checks. No importlib loading or user-controlled module path execution was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports are static module imports guarded by ImportError handling. The code does not construct module names from user input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Access to BRENDA SOAP Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on external BRENDA SOAP access and requires users to register for API credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and authentication flow are documented directly. This is expected but should be disclosed to users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Based Credential Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents .env and environment variable use for BRENDA_EMAIL and BRENDA_PASSWORD.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment credential pattern is explicit. No evidence found of unauthorized credential disclosure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Export Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts write CSV, JSON, Excel, and text report outputs to filenames supplied by callers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly opens caller-selected output files. The behavior is normal for exports but still affects local files.

content.value_statement: Enzyme research often requires manual searches across kinetic data, reactions, organisms, and literature-derived conditions. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code query BRENDA data, parse results, compare enzymes, and prepare pathway analysis.
actual_capabilities: Retrieves BRENDA enzyme kinetic parameters such as Km, kcat, Vmax, and substrate specificity.
Parses BRENDA response entries into organism, substrate, reaction, pH, temperature, and literature fields.
Searches for enzymes by substrate, product, reaction pattern, organism, and EC number.
Compares enzyme properties across organisms and helps identify homologs or stable variants.
Builds pathway and retrosynthesis summaries using enzyme, cofactor, condition, and feasibility data.
Creates plots, exported tables, and pathway reports from retrieved enzyme data.
limitations: Requires valid BRENDA credentials and network access to the BRENDA SOAP service.
Depends on optional Python libraries such as zeep, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, networkx, and requests.
Results depend on BRENDA record coverage and may be sparse for uncommon enzymes or organisms.
Pathway and retrosynthesis outputs are heuristic and require expert biochemical validation.

use_cases.title: Compare Enzyme Kinetics
use_cases.description: Collect Km, kcat, pH, temperature, and substrate data for one EC number across organisms.

use_cases.title: Find Candidate Biocatalysts
use_cases.description: Search BRENDA for enzymes that consume a substrate or produce a target compound.

use_cases.title: Prepare Pathway Evidence
use_cases.description: Build an evidence summary for possible enzymatic steps, cofactors, and operating conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Look Up One Enzyme
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner query for a known EC number.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the BRENDA skill to summarize kinetic parameters, reactions, organisms, and optimal conditions for EC 1.1.1.1.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Organisms
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare BRENDA kinetic and condition data for EC 6.3.5.5 in Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis, and Homo sapiens.

prompt_templates.title: Search by Product
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced discovery for a target compound.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find enzymes in BRENDA that can produce lactate, then rank candidates by organism evidence, kinetic support, and practical conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Build Pathway Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use BRENDA data to draft a retrosynthetic pathway report for ethanol production from glucose with enzymes, cofactors, conditions, and evidence gaps.

output_examples.input: Summarize BRENDA data for alcohol dehydrogenase.
output_examples.output: A concise enzyme profile with known substrates, representative Km values, organisms, reactions, pH ranges, temperature ranges, and missing evidence.
A ranked candidate list showing EC numbers, organisms, reactions, kinetic support, likely cofactors, and notes for experimental follow-up.

output_examples.input: Create a pathway report for a target metabolite.
output_examples.output: A structured pathway summary with proposed steps, enzyme options, feasibility notes, condition constraints, and unresolved data gaps.

best_practices: Verify BRENDA credentials and API access before running large query workflows.
Constrain searches by EC number, organism, substrate, or product to reduce noisy results.
Review exported files and pathway suggestions with domain expertise before using them in experiments.
anti_patterns: Do not treat heuristic pathway suggestions as experimentally validated routes.
Do not store BRENDA credentials in shared repositories or exported reports.
Do not run broad wildcard searches repeatedly without rate limits or result filtering.

faq.question: Does this skill require a BRENDA account?
faq.answer: Yes. BRENDA API access requires registered credentials, usually provided through environment variables or a local .env file.

faq.question: Can it query enzyme kinetics?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports kinetic data such as Km, kcat, Vmax, substrate specificity, and related organism information.

faq.question: Can it build metabolic pathways automatically?
faq.answer: It can draft pathway and retrosynthesis suggestions, but outputs are heuristic and require expert validation.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. The intended workflow contacts the official BRENDA SOAP API to retrieve enzyme data.

faq.question: What files can it create?
faq.answer: The helper scripts can create exported data tables, plots, and text pathway reports when users request those outputs.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
